scenes.title: The scheduler
scenes.tagline: Kubernetes — how a pending pod finds a node
scenes.footnote: pkg/scheduler   ·   a subsystem review — the cycle, the framework, the trade it makes to go fast

beats.narration: The Kubernetes control plane has a scheduler. Its entire job fits in one sentence: take a pod that has no node, and find it one.
It is a subsystem you can read on its own -- one package, p-k-g slash scheduler. A pending pod goes in; a node assignment comes out.
This is a subsystem review -- not a tour of the happy path. The two cycles, the plugin framework, and the trade the scheduler makes to schedule a cluster quickly.

nodes.label: NextPod
nodes.sub: blocks until a pod is ready

beats.narration: Pending pods wait in the SchedulingQueue. It is really three queues -- an active queue of pods ready to try, a backoff queue for pods that just failed, and a holding area for pods already found unschedulable.
NextPod blocks until the active queue has something. That is the heartbeat -- one pod handed forward, then the next.
Every pod runs two cycles. The scheduling cycle picks a node. The binding cycle commits that choice. Hold onto the fact that they are drawn as two separate boxes -- the whole design turns on it.
Two things feed the cycles. The framework -- the plugins that actually decide. And the cache -- the scheduler's own snapshot of every node, so it never has to ask the API server mid-decision.

scenes.heading: Scheduling is a pipeline of plugins

beats.narration: The scheduler does not hard-code its algorithm. It runs a pipeline of extension points, and plugins register at each one. First, Filter -- it asks, for every node in the cluster, can this pod run here at all.

If Filter rejects every single node, PostFilter runs. PostFilter is where preemption lives -- the scheduler considers evicting lower-priority pods to make room. A failure path, promoted to a first-class stage.
If nodes survive Filter, Score ranks them. Every Score plugin returns a number from zero to one hundred for each node; the scheduler sums them and picks the winner.

Then Reserve, Permit, Bind -- claim the node, optionally wait, write the result. Every stage you have seen is a plugin interface. That is the central choice: the scheduler is a framework, not an algorithm -- and the bill for that flexibility is a deep call stack on the hottest path in the cluster.

scenes.heading: A pod through the two cycles

beats.narration: The cycle begins. NextPod hands the scheduler a pod whose node name is empty.

message.label: NextPod() — a pod with no node

beats.narration: The scheduler runs the framework against its cached node snapshot -- Filter, then Score.
The framework returns one node -- the suggested host.
Now the critical move. The scheduler does not call the API server. It writes the pod onto that node in its own cache -- it assumes the bind.
And with the pod assumed, the scheduling cycle is done. Binding is launched in a goroutine -- the scheduler is already free to pull the next pod.
Only now, off the critical path, does the binding cycle call the API server to make the assignment real.
The API server records it; the assume is now true rather than a guess. If that bind had failed, the assumed pod would have to be torn back out of the cache.

scenes.heading: Schedule serially, bind concurrently

beats.narration: Here is the whole shape of it, in ScheduleOne. The scheduling cycle is one synchronous call -- one pod, picked start to finish, before another can begin.
If the cycle fails -- no node fits, a plugin errors -- the scheduler does not fall over. The failure handler re-queues the pod, onto the backoff queue, to be tried again later.
And the payoff is one keyword: go. The binding cycle runs in a goroutine. The moment it is launched, ScheduleOne returns and the next pod's cycle starts -- binding and scheduling now overlap.

Read the comment on line twelve. We can do this because of the assumption step above. That parenthetical is load-bearing -- it is the entire reason the goroutine is safe. The next scene is about what it costs.

Start with the constraint. The scheduling cycle is single-threaded -- one pod fully placed before the next begins. That is deliberate: it means a pod always sees a consistent node snapshot. But it also means this one thread is the cluster's scheduling throughput ceiling.

So the slow part -- the API call to bind -- was pushed off the cycle into a goroutine. The scheduler chose overlapping throughput over a simpler, fully synchronous model. Binding a pod no longer makes the next pod wait.

But a goroutine cannot bind a pod the next cycle has not accounted for. So the scheduler assumes -- it writes the pod onto the node in its own cache immediately, before the API server has agreed. For a window of time, the cache is ahead of the truth.

And that is the real risk. A guess can be wrong. If the bind fails, the scheduler must roll the assume back -- release the resources at once -- or the cache leaks a pod that was never placed, and every later decision is made against a node that looks fuller than it is. Correctness here does not rest on the assume. It rests on the rollback.

scenes.heading: The scheduler, scored
points: One job, done as a pipeline — Filter, Score, Bind are plugin extension points, not a fixed algorithm.
Speed is a trade — schedule serially, bind in a goroutine, assume the bind succeeds.
The cost — the scheduling cycle is single-threaded; that one thread is the cluster's scheduling throughput limit.
The assumed-pod cache is correct only because a failed bind rolls it back — a guess with a safety net.
Past ~5,000 nodes — reach for scheduler profiles or sharding before the one cycle saturates.

beats.narration: So -- the scorecard. What the scheduler gets right: it turned a hard problem into a pipeline. Filter, Score, Bind are extension points, not a fixed algorithm -- you can change scheduling behavior without forking the scheduler.
How it goes fast: a deliberate trade. Schedule pods one at a time, but bind them in the background, and assume each bind will succeed so the next pod need not wait.
And the cost of that trade, said plainly: the scheduling cycle runs one pod at a time. That single thread is the entire cluster's scheduling throughput limit -- no amount of hardware widens it.
The fragile seam: the assumed-pod cache is only correct because a failed bind rolls it back. The optimism is safe, but only as far as that rollback is.

And so, when not to lean on it unchanged: past roughly 5,000 nodes, or under a flood of pods, the one cycle saturates. That is the point to reach for separate scheduler profiles, or to shard scheduling across more than one scheduler -- before the ceiling is the outage.
